title: Influence of glutathione S-transferase polymorphisms on cognitive functioning effects induced by p,p'-DDT among preschoolers.
ehr_evidence: None The study used data from a prospective population-based birth cohort, not EHR. No EHR mentioned; data were collected via questionnaires administered in person, cord serum analysis, and neuropsychological testing performed by psychologists. We obtained information on birth weight, birth length, and gestational age through medical records. None; data were derived from cord serum levels, PCR, pyro-sequencing, and neurodevelopmental scores. None The study uses a specific research cohort (Menorc
summary: The study aims to investigate the relationship between cord serum levels of p,p'-DDT/p,p'-DDE and GST gene polymorphisms as effect modifiers on infant neurodevelopment at 4 years of age.
key_findings: Not provided in the text snippet

p,p´-DDT cord serum concentration was inversely associated with general cognitive, memory, quantitative, and verbal skills, as well as executive function and working memory, in children with any GSTP1 Val-105 allele.

Significant interaction between GSTP1 polymorphisms and prenatal p,p´-DDT exposure for general cognitive skills (p=0.05), quantitative skills (p=0.02), executive function (p=0.01), and working memory (p=0.02).
No significant associations were found between p,p´-DDT and cognitive functioning according to GSTM1 and GSTT1 polymorphisms.
Children with the GSTP1 Val-105 allele were at higher risk of adverse cognitive functioning effects from prenatal p,p'-DDT exposure.
Statistically significant interaction between GSTP1 polymorphisms and prenatal p,p'-DDT exposure for general cognitive skills, quantitative skills, executive function, and working memory.
Comparison of the distribution of child and maternal variables and GST genotypes between included and not included preschoolers.
No significant differences in p,p'-DDT and p,p'-DDE cord serum concentrations between included and excluded children.
MCSA scores were slightly higher in included children than excluded children, but the difference was not statistically significant.
GSTP1 genotype significantly modified the effects of prenatal p,p´-DDT exposure on cognitive functioning.
Adverse effects were restricted to children carrying any GSTP1 Val-105 allele.
Significant interactions found for general cognitive, quantitative skills, executive function, and working memory.

Children with the GSTP1 Val-105 variant showed significant inverse associations between p,p´-DDT cord levels and several neurodevelopmental measures, including Quantitative, Executive function, and Working memory.
Most associations between cord serum p,p´-DDT/DDE and neurodevelopmental measures were non-significant.
Significant associations were found for perceptual performance in specific genotype groups.
Children with the GSTP1 Val-105 allele are more susceptible to the cognitive effects of early-life p,p'-DDT exposure.
The neurotoxic effects of p,p'-DDT may be mediated through oxidative stress.
Neurotoxic effects of p,p'-DDT might be mediated through oxidative stress.
An 8-point decrement in the cognitive scale was observed.
